---
title: "Chapter 3: Customer Clustering Across Three Utilities"
bibliography: ../../references.bib
---

Part 4 Chapter 2 plotted a handful of AusNet's 342 real households side by side
and made one plain observation: no two profiles agree. Some households
barely draw half a kilowatt all day. Others spike past five kilowatts
every evening. That chart was true, and also incomplete. A {{< acr DSO >}}
cannot plan a feeder, price a tariff, or run a demand-response program
around 342 individual shapes. It needs a smaller number of real,
recurring patterns underneath that mess, not an average that describes no
one.

Chapter 1 named the four real questions behind that need. Chapter 2
settled how to answer them without labels: cluster on shape, not
magnitude, and validate the result against resampling rather than a
single index. Whatever recipe wins still has to check out against more
than one real population before anyone trusts it. This chapter is where
that settled methodology meets AusNet's own real customers for the first
time. It builds archetypes from AusNet's own year of real smart-meter
data. It checks whether a customer's pattern is a stable trait or a
one-day snapshot, a question the literature Chapter 1 reviewed never
asks. And it tests whether knowing a customer's pattern predicts
anything a {{< acr DSO >}} would care about. Specifically, which real
{{< acr DER >}} constraint, voltage or thermal, a customer's own future
{{< acr PV >}} or {{< acr EV >}} adoption is likely to trigger.

## Applying a settled methodology, not re-deriving one

Chapter 1 already settled why this is a clustering problem, not a
classification one. No utility keeps a ground-truth "true customer type"
label, so there is structure to discover here, not a known answer to
check against. Part 4 Chapter 3's phase-identification work had a real
answer to check its own clustering against. This chapter does not, and
leans on the same validity indices and honest sensitivity checks Chapter
1 introduced and Chapter 2 put into real practice.

Chapter 1 and Chapter 2 also already settled what "similar" means for a
load shape. Every method in this part clusters on peak-normalized shape,
not raw magnitude. A small apartment and a large house can then land in
the same archetype when their rhythm matches, shown directly in Chapter
1's own figure. Part 4 Chapter 3 used the same principle for a different
reason, tracking phase membership through voltage correlation rather
than raw voltage. This chapter applies that settled choice. It does not
re-derive it.

Chapter 1's own literature review already covers this ground in depth,
from McLoughlin, Kwac, and Haben's early shape-based work through
Rajabi's comparative benchmarking to Michalakopoulos's explainable
clustering and Yerbury's CROCS. None of that literature asks whether a
customer's archetype holds up across a full real year, or only the one
window each study clustered on. That gap is what this chapter's own
stability check fills, later on.

## Getting the data

This chapter reuses the same real AusNet smart-meter pool Part 4
Chapters 1 through 3 already vendored, no new fetch step needed:

```bash
uv run python scripts/fetch_part4_ausnet_data.py
```

Later in this chapter, a real generalization check against NREL's public
ResStock and ComStock building-stock data needs one further fetch, a
stratified sample of 760 real US buildings across 19 real subtypes,
complementing AusNet's own real data, not replacing it:

```bash
uv run python scripts/fetch_part5_nrel_buildstock_data.py
```

## A real season, not one day

A single day is a noisy read on anyone's routine: a sick day, a weekend,
a one-off late night. Averaging a real customer's own readings across a
season smooths that out while staying specific to that customer, not a
population average.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#5e9c50df >}}

## A baseline worth checking, not assuming

The first step is k-means. Rather than assuming the number of clusters,
this chapter checks it with three validity indices together: the elbow
method on inertia, the silhouette coefficient, and the Davies-Bouldin
index, the same combination McLoughlin et al. and Rajabi et al. recommend
checking together, since no single index reliably picks the right k alone
[@mcloughlin2015clustering; @rajabi2020comparative].

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#c55f2ee6 >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#c316f933 >}}

The small-multiples view above puts each index on its own scale. Normalizing
all three to a common 0-1 range and overlaying them makes the same trends
easier to compare directly: does the elbow in inertia line up with the
silhouette peak, or point somewhere else entirely.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#bcee7ce9 >}}

The data show no clear elbow, and the silhouette coefficient peaks at
k=2 (0.227) then decays smoothly. Real residential behavior does not
split into a small number of cleanly separated groups, a genuine finding
about this data, not a modeling failure. k=5 is used from here on, past
the strict silhouette optimum (0.163 at k=5), trading a small amount of
statistical cleanliness for archetypes specific enough to tell a real
story with, a defensible, common choice in applied clustering work, made
transparently rather than silently.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#6405cdb4 >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#9459cd85 >}}

Five visually distinct rhythms: a sharp early-evening peaker, a gradual
afternoon-into-evening riser, a late-evening peaker with a small morning
bump, and two broadly similar late-night risers, exactly the pair the
modest silhouette score was warning about. The chart makes concrete what
the number only hinted at: some of these archetypes genuinely overlap.

### Does the clustering algorithm matter here too?

Part 5 Chapter 2 found that k-means, Gaussian mixture, and agglomerative
clustering return the identical partition once feature, scaler, and
reducer are already fixed. That finding was checked on London's own
PaCMAP-reduced embedding at $K=2$, not on AusNet's own raw
48-dimensional shape at $K=5$. A different population, a different
dimensionality, and a different $K$ is exactly the kind of change that
could break an inherited assumption rather than confirm it, so it gets
checked directly here, not assumed to travel.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#11a0708c >}}

Unlike London's own clean two-way split, the clusterer choice is not
free here. k-means and Gaussian mixture come close, nearly identical
silhouette (0.163 against 0.162) and balance (0.975 against 0.972), and
a real but imperfect agreement (Adjusted Rand Index 0.876). Agglomerative
clustering diverges more. Its silhouette drops to 0.129 and it agrees
with k-means on barely half the population (Adjusted Rand Index 0.456).
Five overlapping archetypes leave real room for algorithms to disagree,
room a cleanly separated two-way split simply does not have. k-means
remains this chapter's own reasonable, defensible choice. It is not
interchangeable with every other family the way Chapter 2's own
easier case suggested.

Choosing five archetypes over the strict silhouette optimum of two was a
deliberate trade, made transparently earlier in this section rather than
silently. Part 5 Chapter 2's own trust-gated composite score put a real
number on exactly this kind of trade: separation bought at the cost of
resampling trust does not average out, it gets vetoed. The same real,
well-powered check that chapter built, prediction strength across ten
held-out splits and cluster-wise stability across a hundred bootstrap
resamples, run here against AusNet's own five-archetype split, prices
that trade honestly instead of leaving it as a qualitative aside.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#3d92fcd5 >}}

A prediction strength of 0.408 sits well under Tibshirani and Walther's
own suggested 0.8 to 0.9 range, the real cost of choosing five archetypes
for the story they tell rather than the two a stricter resampling
standard would prefer. The minimum cluster-wise stability, 0.693, lands
inside Hennig's own "patterned, not stable" band, not the "dissolved"
one below it: these five archetypes are not statistical noise, but they
are not the resampling-bulletproof structure Part 5 Chapter 2's own
settled recipe found for London either. Both readings are the honest
price of choosing operational richness over the maximally trustworthy
split, not a flaw hiding behind the validity indices already reported
above.

## How much does that overlap actually matter?

"Some overlap" is a qualitative read on a chart. Turning it into a number
a {{< acr DSO >}} could actually use borrows a tool Part 4 Chapter 3 already
built: split conformal prediction. There, it checked phase assignments
against a known answer, flagging which customers' labels deserved a
second look. This chapter has no known answer to check against, but the
same tool still works, aimed at a different question: not "is this label
correct," but "how many archetypes could this customer plausibly belong
to."

The method fits k-means on part of the customers and holds the rest back.
For each held-back customer, it measures the distance from that
customer's profile to its own nearest cluster centroid, a real, checkable
number for every customer, not just the ones near a boundary. The held-
back customers' distances pick a threshold, the value 90% of them fall
under. Any customer, old or new, then gets a prediction *set*, not a
single label: every archetype whose centroid sits within that same
threshold.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#69204201 >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#ff2c58e3 >}}

Calibrated on 103 held-back customers, the threshold sits at 1.158. Only
90 of 342 customers (26%) get an unambiguous, single-archetype set. Most
get two, three, or even four plausible archetypes. Twenty customers (6%)
get an empty set: their own best-matching archetype still sits farther
from its centroid than 90% of the held-back customers ever did, real
households whose behavior does not confidently match any of the five
archetypes.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#01fefd41 >}}

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

This is the same split conformal machinery Part 4 Chapter 3 used, aimed at a
different job. There, it flagged phase assignments worth a second look
against a known answer. Here, with no known answer to check against, it
still earns its keep: it turns "some of these archetypes overlap," a
read on a chart, into a checkable, per-customer confidence set. A
{{< acr DSO >}} deciding how much to trust an archetype-based connection
or tariff decision now has a real number to weigh that decision against.

:::

![Three archetype centroids, each surrounded by a circle of radius equal to the calibrated conformal threshold. A customer inside exactly one circle gets a confident single-archetype set; inside two overlapping circles, an honest two-archetype set; outside every circle, an empty set flagging behavior that does not confidently resemble any archetype.](../../assets/cluster-confidence-set.svg){#fig-cluster-confidence-set}

## Does a more advanced method earn its complexity?

IDEC, Improved Deep Embedded Clustering, pretrains an autoencoder, then
jointly optimizes a reconstruction loss and a clustering loss, in
principle learning a representation shaped for clustering rather than
clustering whatever k-means is handed [@guo2017idec]. Kumar and
Mallipeddi apply the same idea to load-pattern segmentation directly,
confirming that lineage is still active [@kumar2024deepclustering].
Worth testing on this exact data before assuming it helps.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#f836f90b >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#78a91d7d >}}

::: {.ark-mistake}
<span class="ark-mistake-title"><i class="bi bi-bug-fill"></i> Common Mistake</span>

**IDEC underperforms plain k-means at every k tested, and the gap widens
as k grows.** This is not a bug. It is what happens when a deep method
meets data too small and low-dimensional to need it: 342 customers, 48
features, is not enough for an autoencoder's extra representational
flexibility to earn its own complexity over a much simpler method already
finding the same structure. The lesson is not "IDEC is bad." It is that a
more advanced method has to prove itself on the data actually in hand,
not get credited for sophistication it does not need here.

:::

## Are these archetypes stable, or a snapshot artifact?

None of this chapter's ten reference papers test this directly. Every one
clusters on a single representative period and treats the result as
fixed. AusNet's own real data is a full year, 365 days per customer, and
most of it goes unused once one season is picked. Re-clustering on
different quarters and comparing the result answers a question none of
the ten papers do: is a customer's archetype a stable trait, or does it
drift?

Checking stability needs a metric first. Chapter 1 already defined the
Adjusted Rand Index ({{< acr ARI >}}) formally: it compares two sets of
cluster labels for the same customers, for every pair asking whether
both label sets agree on putting them together or keeping them apart. A
score of 1.0 means the two labelings agree on every pair. A score of 0.0
means the agreement is no better than what random labels would give by
chance, since {{< acr ARI >}} corrects for that chance level. There is
no ground truth to compare against here, so the two labelings being
compared are
two independent, real clusterings of the same 342 customers, one built
from each quarter's own data. If a customer's archetype is a stable
trait, the quarter-to-quarter clusterings should agree closely. If
archetypes actually drift over the year, they should not.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#458153e0 >}}

Every quarter clusters about as cleanly as the season used above, so
whatever comes next is not an artifact of picking an unusually messy
period. The real test is whether the same customer lands in a matching
archetype across quarters, not just whether each quarter clusters
cleanly on its own.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#71cb776b >}}

The six pairwise {{< acr ARI >}} scores are a summary. The transition
matrix below shows exactly where the disagreement comes from: which
archetype each customer moves to between the first and the last quarter
of the year.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#7eef3a8e >}}

The {{< acr ARI >}} scores are low, between 0.06 and 0.34. The heatmap
shows why: there is no strong diagonal. If archetypes were a fixed trait,
most customers would keep the same label from Q1 to Q4, filling the
diagonal cells. Instead the counts spread across the grid.

Before treating this as real drift, one thing is worth checking first:
how much do the labels move around on their own, just from re-running
k-means on the *same* quarter with a different random seed?

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#e222a411 >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#f230cc27 >}}

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

Same-quarter, different-seed agreement sits at 0.96-0.99. K-means is
highly deterministic on this data, ruling out algorithmic noise as the
explanation. Cross-quarter agreement is 0.06-0.34, an order of magnitude
lower, showing two clearly separated clusters of points in the chart
above. The gap between these numbers is the actual finding: this is not
the clustering method being unreliable. Real customers genuinely change
which archetype they belong to as the year moves on. This is not a
stable trait a utility can safely assume holds from one assessment to
the next.

:::

## Can archetype really predict DER risk?

In Part 4 Chapter 1, three ways {{< acr DER >}} strains a feeder were
identified: fluctuation, load growth, and constraint violations. Chapter
2 showed that {{< acr PV >}} and {{< acr EV >}} adoption bind on
different constraints on this same feeder, voltage first for
{{< acr PV >}}, thermal first for {{< acr EV >}}. If archetype membership
predicts which constraint a customer is likely to trigger, network
planning can shift from reactive, a hosting-capacity study run after a
connection request arrives, to proactive, knowing the risk before it
does.

This is checkable directly. Part 4 Chapter 2's own `run_penetration()` sampled
its 31-customer subset from this exact 342-customer pool with
`np.random.default_rng(42)`. The same seed reproduces exactly which real
customers that study used, so their archetype labels, from the
342-customer clustering above, and their real simulated risk can be
compared directly, not approximated.

There is a solid physical reason to expect this connection, not just
wishful thinking. An archetype describes a customer's own demand shape.
Voltage rise from {{< acr PV >}} depends on how much power flows backward
through the line at the moment {{< acr PV >}} output is highest, usually
midday. A customer who already draws very little at midday leaves more
room for that export before their own local voltage rises too far.
Thermal loading from {{< acr EV >}} charging depends on how much extra
power lands on the transformer at the moment it is already busiest,
usually the evening peak. A customer whose archetype already peaks hard
in the evening adds an {{< acr EV >}}'s charging load right on top of
their own peak, not into a quiet period. If archetype carried no real
information, none of this would show up in the simulation below. If it
does, archetype becomes a genuine planning signal, not just a label.

Two functions do the work. `build_ausnet_network()` rebuilds the same
feeder Chapters 1 and 2 already solved. `per_customer_pv_voltage_risk()`
sweeps a {{< acr PV >}} scenario across it and returns each customer's
own worst-case bus voltage, not only the feeder-wide maximum Part 4 Chapter 2
reported. A matching `per_customer_ev_thermal_contribution()` sweeps an
{{< acr EV >}} scenario and returns each customer's own peak kW draw at
the feeder's own peak-loading instant.

Both scenarios use exactly the same seed and the same sampling call as
Part 4 Chapter 2's own study, so they draw the same 31 real customers.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#8f5ed348 >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#ff56b6fd >}}

Archetype 3 carries the highest mean voltage risk (1.1019 per-unit) but
ranks only fourth of five on mean thermal contribution (8.38 kW).
Archetype 2 carries the highest mean thermal contribution (10.61 kW) but
ranks only second on voltage risk (1.0994 per-unit). Different customer
groups lead different risks on the same feeder, mirroring Part 4 Chapter 2's own
{{< acr PV >}}-versus-{{< acr EV >}} finding, now visible at the
archetype level.

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

Archetype membership carries a real signal, and it does not point the
same way for both {{< acr DER >}} types. A {{< acr DSO >}} that only
checks "which archetype is risky" without asking "risky for which
constraint" would still miss half the picture. The sample is small, 31
customers across five archetypes, so this is a real, checkable
association worth pairing with more data before treating it as settled,
not a large-scale statistical claim.

:::

## A controlled test: can clustering discover a new behavior?

Every check so far describes behavior already sitting in the data. A
{{< acr DSO >}}'s real question is forward-looking: if a specific group
of customers started charging an {{< acr EV >}} tomorrow, would re-
running this same clustering actually notice? That is a testable claim,
not a hypothetical one. Take a real {{< acr EV >}} charging profile,
inject it onto a known set of real households, and check whether
unsupervised clustering rediscovers that group on its own, with no label
telling it where to look.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#9182a404 >}}

Add the real {{< acr EV >}} charging shape directly onto each adopter's
own real kW draw, before normalizing, then re-cluster everyone. Two
versions: k=5, the same number of archetypes used throughout this
chapter, and k=6, one extra slot in case the adopters form a genuinely
new shape rather than just relabeling an existing one.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#8a4d3a19 >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#adcbd769 >}}

The bar chart shows *where* the adopters landed. It does not show *what*
that cluster's shape actually looks like, or whether it resembles a real
{{< acr EV >}} charging bump rather than an existing archetype's own
evening peak.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#636de7d1 >}}

::: {.ark-concept}
<span class="ark-concept-title"><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

Recall is strong: 90% of the injected households land in the same single
cluster at k=5, and 97.5% at k=6. Purity is honest, not perfect: that
cluster is only 38-51% actual adopters, the rest are real households
whose own baseline behavior already looks similar to an evening-charging
{{< acr EV >}} shape. Re-clustering does not perfectly separate every new
adopter from a household that already resembled one, but it reliably
flags where the new behavior concentrates. A {{< acr DSO >}} re-running
this analysis periodically would see this cluster grow and know
something real changed there, even without perfectly identifying every
individual adopter.

:::

## Sensitivity: how much history does one clustering run need?

The stability check above looks at whether an archetype remains
consistent across different situations. But there is another, more
practical question to consider. Within a single season, how much data
does one clustering run actually need to be reliable?

This is important for a {{< acr DSO >}} deciding whether to run this
analysis on just the first week of readings from a new meter instead of
waiting for a full quarter.

They need to know this before considering the stability question. Chapter
3 looked at a similar question about how much data and how many meters
are needed for phase identification. This applies the same approach
here.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#f2c0a5d3 >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#b7dbf2ef >}}

A single day of data hardly matches the season-long baseline. The
{{< acr ARI >}} for one day is 0.02, which is almost random. Even after a
week, the score is still low at 0.11. After a month, the score improves
to 0.35, better, but still not stable. Choosing a season-long window was
not a random decision at the start of this analysis. It marks the point
where the archetype labels stop changing on their own, before the
cross-quarter stability question above even gets asked. A {{< acr DSO >}}
trying to shortcut this analysis with a single day or week of new meter
data would be clustering noise, not behavior.

## Does this generalize beyond AusNet?

Every check so far asks whether an archetype is *stable*, across quarters, across random seeds, across how much history feeds the clustering run. None of them can ask whether an archetype is *right*, because AusNet's own 342 real customers carry no independent, ground-truth label for what kind of customer each one actually is. That is not a gap this chapter can close with more AusNet data, no matter how much of it: the label simply does not exist for these real, anonymous meters.

NREL's real ResStock and ComStock building-stock data, 760 real buildings stratified across 19 real subtypes from single-family homes to hospitals, does carry a genuine ground-truth label: `building_type`, assigned independently of anything this chapter's own clustering could see. Reusing the exact same recipe this chapter already committed to, peak-normalized mean daily shape, `KMeans`, no new method invented for this check, on a population where the right answer is actually known makes a real question checkable for the first time: does clustering by shape alone recover real, known categories, or does it find something else?

<div class='ark-concept'>

<span class='ark-concept-title'><i class="bi bi-info-circle-fill"></i> Key Concept</span>

Chapter 1 already defined both indices this check needs to compare a discovered partition against a known one, each correcting for the agreement chance alone would produce [@hubert1985comparing; @strehl2002clusterensembles]. As a brief reminder, the Adjusted Rand Index counts every pair of objects and asks whether the two partitions agree on whether that pair belongs together, rescaled so a perfect match scores 1 and a chance-level match scores 0:
$$\text{ARI} = \frac{\sum_{ij}\binom{n_{ij}}{2} - \left[\sum_i \binom{a_i}{2}\sum_j \binom{b_j}{2}\right]\big/\binom{n}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\left[\sum_i \binom{a_i}{2} + \sum_j \binom{b_j}{2}\right] - \left[\sum_i \binom{a_i}{2}\sum_j \binom{b_j}{2}\right]\big/\binom{n}{2}}$$
where $n_{ij}$ is the number of objects placed in true group $i$ and discovered group $j$ at once, and $a_i$, $b_j$ are each partition's own group sizes. Normalized Mutual Information asks a different question, how much does knowing one partition reduce real uncertainty about the other:
$$\text{NMI}(U, V) = \frac{I(U, V)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\left[H(U) + H(V)\right]}$$
where $I(U,V)$ is the mutual information between the two partitions and $H(\cdot)$ is Shannon entropy. Neither index rewards a discovered partition just for having a similar number of groups to the true one; both only reward it for actually grouping the same real objects together. Chapter 1's own diagram shows exactly what that means: agreement between two real partitions of the same objects, not just matching group counts.

</div>

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#459405ad >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#510b288c >}}

Every real ARI is close to 0, and every real NMI stays well short of 1, at every k tried, including k=19, matching the true number of subtypes exactly. Shape-based clustering, the same method that gave AusNet's own five archetypes real, checkable value earlier in this chapter, does not recover NREL's real building-type labels here. This is not the same question as before: AusNet's 342 customers are homes and homes only, so real archetypes there reflect genuine behavioral diversity within one functional category. NREL's 760 buildings span single-family homes, hospitals, warehouses, and schools at once, categories that plausibly differ in more than daily shape alone, and shape-based clustering alone is not enough to cleanly separate them.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#f76c2134 >}}

The clusters are not a random shuffle either: one cluster comes out almost entirely commercial, another skews residential, a real, partial structure the low ARI/NMI scores do not fully credit on their own, since those indices score agreement with the full 19-way (or 5-way) label, not the coarser residential/commercial split. The honest reading holds either way: shape alone captures some real structure in a population this diverse, a known building type, occupancy schedule, or floor area would plausibly sharpen it further, and this chapter's own AusNet archetypes should be read as *behavioral* archetypes within one real customer category, not a general-purpose recipe for discovering functional building categories from shape alone.

A table of ARI and NMI numbers states that finding; it does not let a reader see it. Projecting the same 48-dimensional shape embedding this chapter has used throughout down to its first two principal components, the same reduction this book's own `ark.plot.clustering.cluster_scatter` already applies everywhere else a shape embedding needs a 2D picture, turns the abstract "does not recover" verdict into something checkable by eye.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#fe2210aa >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#8c55a16e >}}

Side by side, the two panels show exactly what the ARI/NMI numbers already reported: the five discovered clusters cut across this projection in bands that only loosely track the real residential/commercial boundary. Some discovered clusters sit almost entirely on one side of that boundary, echoing the composition table above; others straddle it, buildings whose daily shape looks similar enough to land in the same cluster despite belonging to genuinely different real categories. No amount of staring at this projection would let a reader guess the true label perfectly from the discovered one, the same limit the ARI of roughly 0.06 to 0.09 already quantified.

The cluster means themselves are the more concrete artifact a {{< acr DSO >}} would actually use, the same member-profiles-and-mean chart this chapter has shown for AusNet's own archetypes throughout, now run on NREL's real, more diverse population.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#47b34867 >}}

These five discovered shapes read as genuinely distinct real behaviors, and the composition table above lines up with them directly. Cluster 1, 97% commercial, shows a sharp, tall midday peak, roughly 10:00 to 14:00, the shape of a business open standard daytime hours. Cluster 4, 92% commercial, peaks narrower still, later in the afternoon. Cluster 0, three-quarters commercial, stays relatively flat and elevated across the entire day, plausible for a round-the-clock operation rather than a nine-to-five one. Cluster 2, the most residential-leaning group at 73%, is the clear outlier of the five: a broader, gentler rise into the afternoon and evening, closer to a home's own daily rhythm than any of the sharp commercial peaks. Cluster 3, the most genuinely mixed group, 40% residential, reads as a broad, low-contrast plateau, no single real behavior dominating clearly, exactly what a 60/40 mix of two different populations sharing one cluster should look like. That is a genuinely useful, real finding on its own: shape-based clustering is not producing noise, it recovers real, interpretable behavioral groups, largely tracking the coarse residential/commercial split even where it does not track the full 19-way label. What it does not reliably do, and what the ARI/NMI numbers above already quantified, is line those groups up with NREL's own independently assigned building-type labels one-to-one. A cluster that looks "sharp midday peak" plausibly contains a real mix of offices, retail spaces, and schools that all happen to draw power on the same daytime schedule for different underlying reasons, a distinction daily shape alone cannot resolve, whatever a known building type, occupancy schedule, or floor area might resolve instead.

## Does this generalize to other utilities?

The NREL check above asks whether shape recovers a *known* label. It is a
different question from whether the recipe itself, peak-normalised
shape, no dimension reduction, $K$ chosen by the same validity indices
this chapter already uses, finds similarly coherent structure on an
independently metered population elsewhere. London and GoiEner, loaded
through the same shared `ark.cluster.datasets` helpers Part 5 Chapter 2's
own cross-utility check reuses, answer that here, letting each
population's own $K$ emerge rather than forcing AusNet's five onto it.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#ee7d137c >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#14e00cd5 >}}

London settles on $K=2$, the same answer Part 5 Chapter 2's own
PaCMAP-reduced search found, even with no dimension-reduction step in the
pipeline at all: a real, independent confirmation that London's own
two-way split is a property of the population itself, not an artefact of
any one embedding. The resampling numbers still move, though. Prediction
strength drops from Chapter 2's own perfect 1.000 to 0.782 here, and
minimum cluster stability from 1.000 to 0.915, both still comfortably
inside Tibshirani and Walther's and Hennig's own trustworthy ranges, but
a real, measurable cost: dimension reduction is not only cosmetic, it
finds a more resampling-robust separation than clustering on the raw
48-dimensional shape directly. GoiEner settles on a genuinely different
$K=4$, its own smallest cluster's stability, 0.796, and prediction
strength, 0.546, both weaker again, consistent with the same pattern
this chapter's own sensitivity section already established: less data,
here a smaller real population and a coarser hourly resolution, buys
less resampling confidence.

Worth being precise about which of Chapter 2's own findings this
reconfirms. That chapter's own search surfaced several real, differently
resolved answers for London, not only $K=2$. A richer six-way split on
the same shape representation passes the same real resampling thresholds
just as convincingly, and so does a genuinely different four-way split
on day-to-day volatility. This section's own no-dimension-reduction
pipeline reconfirms specifically the *coarse* two-way resolution,
independently, on a different embedding entirely. The richer resolutions
remain a PaCMAP-space-specific finding. They are not yet checked against
this chapter's own no-reduction convention the way $K=2$ has been here.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#383ef6fe >}}

Three real populations, three different answers when no dimension
reduction is involved: AusNet's own five, chosen for operational
richness at a real resampling cost already quantified above; London's
two, the same trustworthy split Chapter 2's reduced-space search found
independently; GoiEner's four, its own real answer, not borrowed from
either of the other two. What generalises is the recipe, peak-normalised
shape, real validity-index-driven $K$ selection, a real resampling audit
before trusting the result, not one fixed number of archetypes every
utility is assumed to share.

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#0a98a24d >}}

{{< embed 03-customer-clustering-code.ipynb#ce6c7191 >}}

London's own two archetypes read as cleanly as AusNet's five did, a
sharper evening-peaking group against a flatter, lower one. GoiEner's
four are less crisply separated visually, consistent with its own
weaker resampling numbers above: real structure, read honestly, is not
always as clean as the population that happens to anchor this chapter.

The real {{< acr PV >}}/{{< acr EV >}} {{< acr DER >}}-risk
cross-tabulation earlier in this chapter stays AusNet-network-specific:
it needs the real OpenDSS feeder model Part 4 Chapters 1 and 2 already
solved, and neither London nor GoiEner has a paired real network model in
this book. What generalises here is the clustering recipe itself, not
the {{< acr DER >}}-risk simulation built on top of it.

## Why bother

Part 4 Chapter 1 named three ways {{< acr DER >}} strains a feeder: fluctuation,
load growth, and constraint violations. Every study since Part 4 Chapter 2 has
answered "how much strain, and where" one feeder at a time, after the
fact. This chapter's contribution is different: knowing which customers
are likely to cause which kind of strain, before they ever apply to
connect solar or an {{< acr EV >}} charger.

**On the utility side**, the archetype-versus-risk cross-tabulation above
is a leading indicator, not a retrospective one. Archetype 3 flags
voltage risk; archetype 2 flags thermal risk; neither flag would exist
without clustering customers by behavior first. A {{< acr DSO >}}
triaging a connection queue can prioritize monitoring and reinforcement
spend on the archetypes this chapter actually flags as high-risk, and
pair the right lever from Part 4 Chapter 2, Volt-Watt, Volt-VAr, or storage,
with the right archetype at connection time, instead of a one-size-fits-
all rule applied after a hosting-capacity study finds a problem. The
controlled {{< acr EV >}} injection test adds a second, forward-looking
piece: re-clustering periodically would catch a new adopter cluster
forming with 90-97.5% recall, a real early-warning signal for where
{{< acr DER >}} adoption is concentrating, not just where it already has.

**On the customer side**, the same cross-tabulation cuts the other way. A
customer whose archetype this chapter shows as low-risk can get a faster,
cheaper connection approval. One whose archetype shows real risk can be
paired with the right mitigation up front, storage bundled with the
connection, say, instead of a surprise curtailment discovered months
later. A tariff targeted by real behavioral archetype, not a blanket
residential rate, is a fairer cost-to-serve match for both sides of the
meter.

**The stability finding closes the loop.** This two-sided value only
holds if an archetype-based decision stays valid after it is made. The
conformal confidence sets above already flag which customers' archetype
labels are solid and which are ambiguous at the moment of decision. The
cross-quarter check goes further: it shows the label itself can drift
within a year, 0.06-0.34 agreement against 0.96-0.99 for pure algorithmic
noise. A stable archetype is a trustworthy basis for a connection-
approval or tariff decision that persists. A churning one means last
year's archetype is targeting the wrong households today, a real, costed
risk, repeated re-clustering, or decisions quietly going stale, a
{{< acr DSO >}} would actually have to weigh, not a hypothetical one.

## Where this leaves Part 5

Five real customer archetypes, tested against three separate validity
indices rather than assumed. A deep-clustering contender that honestly
loses to a much simpler method on this data. A conformal confidence set
that turns "some archetypes overlap" into a checkable, per-customer
number. A stability check none of this chapter's ten reference papers
run, showing those archetypes are a real but genuinely time-varying
trait, not a fixed label. A sensitivity check showing why a full season,
not a day or a week, earns that trust. A controlled test showing
clustering can rediscover a real behavior change with 90-97.5% recall.
And a direct, checkable link from archetype to which real {{< acr DER >}}
constraint, voltage or thermal, a customer's own future {{< acr PV >}} or
{{< acr EV >}} adoption is likely to trigger.

These archetypes are not the end of the story. They are a grouping key
later parts of this book reuse, and Chapter 5 takes the same shape-based
recipe one level up, to real feeders rather than real customers. Part 7
builds anomaly-detection baselines
per archetype rather than one baseline for all 342 customers pooled
together. Part 8's own {{< acr DER >}}-growth scenarios, once built, can
apply per archetype rather than uniformly across a feeder, using exactly
the enrichment found above to decide where growth is likely to bind
first.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
